This sentence is not strictly true currently.  ODC 1.8.x allows for non-spatial datasets (e.g. for storing telemetry metadata), which can be searched for but can't be loaded into an xarray with spatial dimensions.  The metadata types for these do not have to have grid_spatial, measurements and format.

But for metadata types for spatial datasets that can be loaded into xarrays with spatial dimensions, this sentence is true.

I'm leaning towards dropping support for non-spatial metadata types/products/datasets in ODCv2, but they will still be at least partially supported into v1.9

I'm just studying this magic method and `__str__' in Python, what I see the convention is:
- `__repr__` is for developers (who develop the lib): it should capture what's deciding this class ("unambiguous"). In this case, it should be base_datasets and stages. Similar for Dataset, it's input_blocks and plan.
- `__str__` is for users (who use the lib): it should make it more readable and user friendly, such as schema, num of windows (I think stages is optional to mention). For Dataset, it's current `__repr__` which print schema and num of rows etc (which are user oriented).

Btw, my feeling is that the logic of `skipna` is wrong in this implementation. For pandas it is 
>Apply the specified dropna operation before counting which row is the nth row. Only supported if n is an int.

In this implementation what we do is return the first non-missing after the n-th row. However, we don't remove missings until the n-th row.

```python
>>> DT = dt.Frame([None, 1, None])
>>> DT[:, dt.nth(f[:], n=0, skipna=True)]
   |   C0
   | int8
-- + ----
 0 |    1
[1 row x 1 column]
>>> DT[:, dt.nth(f[:], n=1, skipna=True)]
   |   C0
   | int8
-- + ----
 0 |    1
[1 row x 1 column]
>>> DT[:, dt.nth(f[:], n=2, skipna=True)]
```

`**_kwargs` is fine if you like that better, I think it was complaining about it being unused. `**kwargs` means you are using the extra arguments, usually - the convention is arguments that start with an underscore might be unused, but normal arguments always will be used. It could also be an ignore comment, but I think `_` or `_kwargs` conveys the "we won't use it" meaning better than a comment and an unused `kwargs`. But not strongly opinionated on it; it's so short it's hard to miss that it's unused.

This is different from the use of "unknown" in ArrayBuilder. Every ArrayBuilder starts as unknown and any operation is valid on it because ArrayBuilder changes its type; TypedArrayBuilder doesn't change type, so no operation is valid on an unknown type.

(It wouldn't be particularly useful to use EmptyForms with TypedArrayBuilder; it's just here for completeness. I don't think these points are pedantic-- I think it's important to be very precise when talking about machine learning. IMO there's enormous confusion among people because not enough authors have been pedantic enough! 

This is framed as "model architecture selection" bc this is the most accurate description I came up with of what we are actually doing. Here's why:

As you correctly point out, our ultimate goal is **model selection**. However, the naive approach to this of "training a bunch of models with a bunch of architectures/hyperparameters and [using] the best one" is biased because it overfits the training data. You can't get away from this problem, and so you shouldn't select model with this straightforward method.

Instead, *the rigorous way to do model selection is via model architecture selection.* You train a bunch of models, evaluate the performance of these models as a means of selecting the best *model architecture*, and then train a single final model (using the optimal architecture) on your entire training set (none of the models used to evaluate candidate architectures are trained on the whole training set). That final model is your answer to the model selection problem.

Also ,I'm open to using a different term than "model architecture", and I agree that it is somewhat ambiguous-- unfortunately there does not seem to be a better available term of art for this important concept: a fully-configured (i.e. all hyperparameters specified) learning algorithm. Part of the ambiguity arises from the fact that  hyperparameters often (e.g. number of hidden units in a neural network) map directly to "spatial", i.e. architectural, characteristics of the algorithm (unlike learning rate). In any case, this very ambiguity is part of the reason the background is as long as it is. With no unambiguous dominant lexicon, I have to set up clear meanings for these terms within the article scope.

I'm not sure it's a good idea to recreate the whole Router API here as a dummy implementation.  It seems unlikely to stay up to date as we modify the actual Router, unless TypeScript could enforce it for us, and I'm guessing it can't at least in this context.

But more broadly it seems like Router isn't really the right abstraction here.  IMO one of the goals of the graph visualizer is that it's a chunk of self-contained visualization code that doesn't require TensorBoard APIs to work correctly and can be hosted outside TensorBoard; any TensorBoard-specific logic should added just in `<tf-graph-dashboard>`.

It looks like #1853 introduced the dependency from `<tf-graph-loader>` to the Router.  Could we rework that change so we use a smaller, graph-visualizer-specific abstraction instead?  E.g. we could create a simple graph-specific variation on Router that just provides paths for the `/graph` and `/run_metadata` endpoints (and nothing else).  Or maybe we could have the loader take pre-constructed URLs within its `selection` rather than constructing them on-the-fly in `_load()`.

Rephrased this in a more iteratory way, but it still has an `unwrap` because the ordering of the checks we're doing makes it hard to encode the required information cleanly into the type system.

We want to say:
1. Maybe connect (iterate to find a connectable)
2. Else maybe reuse
3. Else iterate to find what will next be connectable

If we were happy to unconditionally do the next finding in the maybe-connect stage, even if we may end up reusing a connection, we could phrase this as:

1. Iterate over all servers, finding the next one we should try to connect to
2. If we can make a connection, and the server is healthy, connect to it
3. If nothing is connectable, and we have an open connection, re-use it
4. Else reschedule for when something will be connectable

But because we don't want to do the iteration and time calculations in step 1 if step 2 is going to fail and we're going to use step 3, we can't just write this as one cute expression.

@jianoaix I understand that general pattern when the logging is centralized, but how valuable is a per-worker `log_every_n()` or `log_every_n_seconds()` going to be when the logging is going to happen once per stage but for every worker?

Since we don't want to introduce expensive coordination for the sake of log throttling, the options are:
1. Log once per worker per stage (no local logging guard).
2. Log once per worker (current local logging guard).
3. Log every once in a while per worker (your proposed local "log every n (seconds)").

I'm thinking that we want either (1), which gives complete information to the user and allows them to identify stages in which this is happening, or (2), which lets the user know that this problem exists but doesn't spam them with logs, and I'm not sure what value the compromise (3) would give... maybe that would reduce the amount of spam when doing batch inference over a size-constrained actor pool while still periodically letting the user know that this failed cast is happening, but I feel like (2) is still better since (3) could end up spamming the user for a long-running batch inference stage.

The thresholding criterion we apply in order to determine if the two point sets have sufficiently low RMSD (i.e. "match") is quite conservative: the two point sets match if and only if, for every pair of matched points between the sets, the distance between the matched pair is less than :code:`threshold`.

|---|---| --- |---|
|Update By|head|stats_collector|reporter|
|Interval Seconds| 5 |  1(this one) | 2.5 |

I'm not a huge fan of this layout, where these fields are required in one case (SQL) and disallowed (ignored and must not be set) in another case (non-SQL). I'm worried this could end up causing additional complexity and allow bugs to creep in. For example, we'll have to validate these fields differently depending on the backend, which means that having constructed `SchemaInfo` instance does not necessarily mean that the instance is valid and usable.

Instead, consider a `SchemaInfo[BackendSpecificDataT]` generic dataclass, where the `BackendSpecificDataT` can contain different things for different backends. We may then be able to use `NewType` from [typing](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#newtype) to create "new" types for each of the few combinations of `SchemaInfo` and backend-specific data. Not sure if this solution is ideal either — there may be something better still. But it seems to have fewer risks than this option, where `SchemaInfo` carries all the backend-specific fields directly, even when they are not allowed to be used.

Introducing new credential/policy vs updating existing AzureKeyCredential/AzureKeyCredentialPolicy. Currently in the AzureKeyCredential/AzureKeyCredentialPolicy, users store the API key in the credential and the header in the policy. In the two header-api key scenarios, we need to store the header and corresponding api key in one place (we also need to store the mapping information). Hence, we need to make non-trivial changes on the surfaces. E.g. the policy ctor does not require the header any more, credential.update() needs the header in addition to the value, etc.. Which makes me think it is better and simpler to introduce a new one.

I'm obviously not explaining my point well.  The problem with the code in the linked example is this:

```python
def get_os_version_codename(codename, version_map=OPENSTACK_CODENAMES):
    '''Determine OpenStack version number from codename.'''
    for k, v in six.iteritems(version_map):
        if v == codename:
            return k
    e = 'Could not derive OpenStack version for '\
        'codename: %s' % codename
    error_out(e, raiser=ValueError)
```

The last part *changes the behaviour of* `get_os_version_codename()` which other charms rely on.  This means that `get_os_version_codename()` now raises a `ValueError`, and doesn't do `sys.exit()` because `error_out()` is also changed.  These are identical in the `get_os_version_codename()` function as changed above.

```python
error_out(e, raiser=ValueError)
# or
raise ValueError(str(e))
```
Which makes it worse as it now redefines what error_out does, which is no longer "erroring out of the hook", but "raise a ValueError instead, if told".  Semantically, these things are different and *will* lead to confusion in the future.  It matters what things are named and that this matches what the thing does.

That's the problem.  Please don't change the default behaviour of `get_os_version_codename()`.

Adding a subnet is not as simple as adding a new resource. We need to take the **vnet's address space** and **subnet's prefix** into consideration. 

It is very hard to deduce the **new subnet's prefix** based on the **existing vnet's address space** and **existing subnet's prefix**. Even Azure Portal doesn't do the decision for the user. When creating a subnet, it just leave the field `Address range (CIDR block)` empty.

Some example edge cases:

1. If the existing vnet has address space `10.3.0.0/16`, this command will try to create a subnet with default prefix `10.0.0.0/24` and will fail, because the prefix is not in the allowed address space:
    > Azure Error: NetcfgInvalidSubnet
    > Message: Subnet 'vm3Subnet' is not valid in virtual network 'vnet3'.

2. If the existing vnet's address space is the same as the existing subnet's prefix - both `10.0.0.0/16`, the subnet creation will fail in any case, because there is no space left

3. If the existing vnet's address space is `10.0.0.0/24`, how do we decide the mask of the subnet? 24, 25? It is not practical to do the decision for the user

I prefer to error out if vnet is specified while subnet is not.

```py
from mcstatus.status_response import DeprecatedJavaStatusResponse as PingResponse
```
which can then be easily removed after the deprecation period and simply replaced by
```py
from mcstatus.status_response import JavaStatusResponse
```

This is needed because some people may want to use the new classes introduced here in their code directly by importing them. Keeping the classes prefixed with `New` would mean that after the deprecation period, we would have to keep using the `New` prefixed classes, even though there won't be any old/original classes, and so that name wouldn't make any sense.

`sys.path` should not be reassigned in that manner because `sys.path` is a mutable object that may have been imported somewhere else prior to being manipulated here. Any namespace that has already imported `sys.path` will continue to point to the original memory address location, while subsequent imports will point to the new `sys.path`, thus `sys.path` should be manipulated "in place". Both `.extend()` and `.insert()` methods do this.

```python
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Generic, TypeVar

T = TypeVar('T')


class FieldValue(Generic[T]):
    def __init__(self, value: T):
        self.value = value


if __name__ == '__main__':
    field1 = FieldValue(value=123)
    field2 = FieldValue(value='hello')
    field3 = FieldValue(value=datetime.utcnow())

    a = field1.value
    b = field2.value
    c = field3.value
```

This part exists because we have imaging data in "HST" coordinates, and kinematic data in "MUSE" coordinates, which you can relate to one another through RA and DEC. Furthermore, we have the lens model, which is in HST coordinates (imaging model), and might be off center or with a misaligned PA compared to this coordinate systems. The NN returns a model aligned with the x axis, so this must be rotated into the same as the MUSE system, but that rotation and translation depends on the lens model PA, which relates it to the HST system, then to RA/DEC, then to the MUSE system to be interpolated.

I see, you're right. The problem is a little different from what I described above. Here's what's actually happening to cause that unit test to fail:

1. The test defines a scheduler that manually calls `tune_controller.pause_trial(trial)` during the **`on_trial_result`** hook
2. `should_checkpoint=True` by default, so this will schedule a SAVE and set the cached trial decision here:

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/3984b8547eb34938ad114212f8dd1662f8ac6b06/python/ray/tune/execution/tune_controller.py#L1631

3. At this point, we return a NOOP trial scheduler decision (since we paused manually) and end up here:

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/3984b8547eb34938ad114212f8dd1662f8ac6b06/python/ray/tune/execution/tune_controller.py#L1767-L1768

4. The trial IS SAVING at this point, so we enter this block:

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/3984b8547eb34938ad114212f8dd1662f8ac6b06/python/ray/tune/execution/tune_controller.py#L1806-L1820

5. We overwrite the `PAUSE` cached decision with the `NOOP`, leading to an infinite hang.

Thinking about this more, I feel even more strongly that we should make this change.

I think the current approach is going to cause us problems, now and in the future.

You can already see one problem forming in this PR, where there's a bunch of places in the code where we need to manually concat the scope and templateKey to form the object_id.

Another place we're going to run into problems is with POST and PUT operations where the body expects metadata template. If we naively do `template.object_id`, that might be incorrect if the API expects the actual id (I know of at least one API that was supposed to be doing this in the future). If we do `template.id`, that field might not actually be available, if it wasn't constructed with one.

For simplicity, we could make it so that if we're constructed with the id, we fetch the scope and templateKey, and also vice-versa, that way we always have all of those pieces of information available.

I had a hard time grocking it's intended use case but after some experimentation it appears to handle something like this well where there is a shared resource (the tip peer), something in charge of managing it (the `ChainTipMonitor`), and many subscribers that want to be notified when it's available.

Nothing wrong with this API though, just something you might investigate.

With only some minor changes, you could fit this functionality in `_datetime_impl.py` and saving a new module:

```python
RFC3339_DATE_PATTERN = '%Y-%m-%d'
RFC3339_TIME_PATTERN = '%H:%M:%S'
RFC3339_DATETIME_PATTERN = '{}T{}'.format(
    RFC3339_DATE_PATTERN, RFC3339_TIME_PATTERN)


def to_str(
        dt: Union[datetime, date],
        strip_microseconds: bool,
        fork_inst: type,
        pattern: str = RFC3339_DATETIME_PATTERN) -> str:
    offset = get_offset_str(dt, fork_inst)
    if not strip_microseconds and getattr(dt, 'microsecond', None):
        pattern += '.%f'
    return dt.strftime("{}{}".format(pattern, offset))


def get_offset_str(
        obj: Union[datetime, date, timedelta],
        fork_inst: type) -> str:
    """
    Return the textual offset of the given ``obj``.
    :param obj: a datetime or timedelta instance.
    :param fork_inst: the state holder that is used.
    :return: the offset following RFC3339.
    """
    result = ''
    if isinstance(obj, datetime):
        result = _datetime_offset_str(obj, fork_inst)
    elif isinstance(obj, timedelta):
        result = _timedelta_offset_str(obj, fork_inst)
    return result


def get_datetime_inst(obj: str, pattern: str) -> datetime:
    """
    Return a datetime instance with timezone info from the given ``obj``.
    :param obj: the ``obj`` in RFC3339 format.
    :param pattern: the datetime pattern.
    :return: a datetime instance with timezone info.
    """
    if obj[-1] == 'Z':
        result = _datetime_utc_inst(obj, pattern)
    elif 'T' in pattern:
        result = _datetime_offset_inst(obj, pattern)
    else:
        result = datetime.strptime(obj, pattern)
    return result
```

This is how you can use it:

```python
d_in = datetime.now().date()
d_str = to_str(d_in, False, StateHolder, pattern=RFC3339_DATE_PATTERN)
d_out = get_datetime_inst(d_str, RFC3339_DATE_PATTERN)
```

One of the hotly debated topics in C++ is whether you should use references or pointers for member data (see [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/892133/should-i-prefer-pointers-or-references-in-member-data)).

There are a few notable differences between the two approaches that are worth considering:

- References cannot be re-assigned or null, unlike pointers.
- Objects containing references as members are non-assignable (cannot be copied).
- References cannot be lazy initialized.

Personally, I think it's better to use pointers, but it's your call.

It makes sense to me that increasing the collection time will fix flakiness caused by inadequate collection, but I'd like to discuss the explanation a bit.  If I say something agreeable, would you update the comment accordingly?

AFAIK, `time.sleep()` as of Python 3.5 will auto-restart if the runtime catches a signal.  It also makes the standard provision that the amount slept will be _at least_ as long as the amount specified, possibly longer.  What this means is, the `time.sleep()` on line 45 will be at _least_ as long as two minimum interval times.  In other words, time-variation of this sleep favors "more collection" than would be a priori expected from just the specified times/frequencies alone.

On the other hand, I think what's actually happening here is that rather than getting short-sleeps from this `time.sleep()`, we're actually getting _long_ collection intervals from `ddtrace/profiling/collector/stack.pyx:StackCollector.collect()`.  `collect()` will exacerbate any case where the scheduling mechanism _also_ provides that the sleep is "at least as long" as the one specified, since it'll only collect if the total waited time exceeds the specified interval (and you'll practically never sleep right _on_ the interval).

If this is accurate, then I think we should specify that we're accounting for variations in the collection sampling interval.

This implementation ensures that the heading vector wraps around the edges of the toroidal space correctly, taking into account the shortest distance between the two points. In a toroidal space, each dimension wraps around like a circular loop, so the shortest distance between two points may involve wrapping around the edge.

By adding `self.half_size` to the `heading`, the space is translated so that the center of the torus is at the origin. This makes it easier to perform the modulo operation.

Next, the modulo operation with `% self.size` is applied. This operation ensures that the heading vector stays within the bounds of the toroidal space, wrapping around when it exceeds the size.

Finally, subtracting `self.half_size` translates the space back to its original position.

Simply using `heading % self.size` would not correctly account for the shortest distance between two points in a toroidal space. It would wrap around the edges, but it wouldn't consider cases where the shortest path involves going across the edge, leading to incorrect heading vectors.

Yes, we should absolutely not worry about whether a multiplication is done on the host side or device side, and whether it's done once per thread or once for all. Addition and multiplication are the two fastest operations a processor can do and they're usually vastly overshadowed by time spent fetching memory. On a GPU, the memory-fetching is less significant because global memory is essentially like a CPU's cache—it's close to the processor—but still, it's unlikely that the other work these kernels need to do (especially the parallel prefix sum ones) will be close to the speed of multiplication.

Also, it's not "structural," we can easily change it in the future, in response to profiling tests. So it's not something that we'll worry about right now.

Hi @ashione, here are more context. If we define `ReportMetrics(MetricPoints, OpenCensusData)`. The issue here are two folds:
- MetricPoints are derived from OpenCensusData. This means if we only need OpenCensus data, the MetricsPoints computation is redundant. (For the MetricExporterClient implementations, the `ReportMetrics` are implemented in Either-Or.)
- `MetricPoints` has different length than `OpenCensusData` because the distribution values from `OpenCensusData` are expanded into max, min, and average. This makes it difficult for batching.

Additionally, the MetricExporterClient makes it possible to just override one method: `ReportMetrics(MetricPoints)` and leave the OpenCensus on unimplemented. This marks it clear the `MetricPoints` are the only implementation required for all sublcasses and use the decorator chain to pass on the data. `ReportMetrics(OpenCensusData)` is entirely optionally and fall back to empty implementation block (Noop with a warning). `ReportMetrics(OpenCensusData)` will not go through the decorator chain.

As I commented previously in the `getattr_missing_optional_dep` case, for a library (like QtPy) used by many downstream applications, "private"/"internal" _to the package_ (i.e. QtPy, it is not intended for outside consumers of the library to use) is the much more relevant (and important) meaning here than "private"/"internal" _to the module_ (as would be the case for an application like Spyder):

> It's private to the _package_ as a whole rather than the specific module its defined in. At present, this means it is exposed as a module-level public function in any of the modules its imported in. Furthermore, its not intended to be use by anything except as an internal implementation detail of the common `__getattr__` implementation.

Therefore, like the `getattr` helper, IMO it is entirely appropriate that it be `_` private, as it is only intended for strictly internal use rather than being exposed to users.

Perhaps, though, we could just mark the `utils` module private instead, since was specifically created for such "private"/"internal" utils.

Given the restriction that is applied here (that the yield is always a trailing statement in a block), I don't think accidental early return is likely. And there is nothing illegal about a coroutine that is "partially consumed". While the semantics of "if I yield a Get, I should expect to resume, otherwise, I can expect to exit" doesn't take much time to pick up... and the check that Danny has added helps to ensure that you don't get those swapped.

I guess I had two things in mind with this behaviour. One is the behaviour if you have a typo or copy-paste error. It seemed more useful that you'd get a loud error if you are trying to use the body of a message when that body doesn't exist at all.

The other was the situation of a higher level library that wants to implement some kind of fallback to a default locale. If every error condition returns some value (e.g. "???") it becomes harder to know when to fallback.

I imagined that for this condition, it is very likely there is some kind of developer/tranlsator error (e.g. in v1 of your translation files you had a message without a message body, in v2 it gains a message body but some of the FTL files have not been updated). So in this case you would want fallback to a different locale. 

Hopefully that also helps convince that this is the right thing (though I'm certainly open to persuasion the other way).

Interesting! So I actually think the issue in this case isn't the fact that we didn't print the error code!

The usage of `ddtrace-run` is that you prefix it to your normal Python command (eg. `python file.py`). So in @jd's case it was actually expected behaviour because if he had done `./file.py` it would have failed as well with `zsh: permission denied: ./file.py`, or similar. (I think `pyddprofile` worked differently by allowing you to exclude `python` which is probably why @jd ran into this issue).

I had added this handling here because the most common misusage of `ddtrace-run` was exactly what @jd did but the usage message wasn't clear enough to correct the usage - which is what I think the problem actually is in this case!

This is what `ddtrace-run` did before the way it is now:

```
$ ddtrace-run file.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/private/tmp/.venv3.7/bin/ddtrace-run", line 8, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/private/tmp/.venv3.7/lib/python3.7/site-packages/ddtrace/commands/ddtrace_run.py", line 82, in main
    os.execl(executable, executable, *sys.argv[2:])
  File "/Users/kyle.verhoog/.pyenv/versions/3.7.7/lib/python3.7/os.py", line 542, in execl
    execv(file, args)
PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied
```

This is what is does now:

```
$ ddtrace-run file.py
ddtrace-run: executable 'file.py' does not have executable permissions: [Errno 13] Permission denied

usage: ddtrace-run <your usual python command>
```

The problem with it is probably the `<your usual python command>`. I think if we had a few examples like `python file.py`, `gunicorn file.py` and `file.py (if executable)` underneath then it'd be much more helpful.


Now with all that being said, I think @jd brings up a good point that we shouldn't hide the error information from the user (especially those that don't trust us 😆). The intention here was certainly not to outsmart the user but rather to be more helpful than just throwing an error at them. I think the solution is to do both: print the error as well as the usage information.

Something about this rubbed me the wrong way, so I dug in some more. The initial code, which used `account` as *username* if `login` is empty, came from requests (https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/ac3be98b19f4d09c6a970b271a3ae30f3d0858f7/requests/utils.py#L238).

However, I actually believe requests is *wrong* here. If you look at the [inetutils netrc docs](https://www.gnu.org/software/inetutils/manual/html_node/The-_002enetrc-file.html), it says:

>  Supply an additional account password. If this token is present, the auto-login process will supply the specified string if the remote server requires an additional account password, or the auto-login process will initiate an ACCT command if it does not.

So it seems to be used as an additional **password**, not as username. I looked up what `ACCT` is, and it seems to be the FTP ACCT command (https://cr.yp.to/ftp/user.html), allowing users to have multiple 'subaccounts' of sorts inside a single login. However, note that even with FTP, you can not use 'ACCT' independent of 'LOGIN'.

I looked at some other clients - curl doesn't even recognize `account` (https://everything.curl.dev/usingcurl/netrc for docs, https://github.com/curl/curl/blob/07dfbc08bfb4322b257d6fae775451ec58e63c80/lib/netrc.c#L65 for code). Neither does httpx (https://github.com/encode/httpx/blob/e5bc1ea533aa5cfcc4f7b179e9faa27f689ed91f/httpx/_utils.py#L152). 

So my suggestion here is to actually *not* follow requests, but instead to follow curl and just ignore the `account` field entirely, as sort of being irrelevant to the HTTP protocol. This would also affect existing users (although I doubt it) though, so if you consider it a breaking change, am happy to submit it as a separate PR that can go into 4.x. I am also happy to keep the status quo - although the digging here was mostly because I realized I could ask myself 'wait, *why* are we prefering login?'.

```python
from azure.storage.blob import BlockBlobService

blob_client = BlockBlobService(is_emulated=True)  # will automatically use the default local connection string and keys
```

>might be confused with simple type definition

The `eth_typing` as well as it's little sister `eth.typing` aren't exclusively for `NewType` or *type aliases* if that's what you mean by "type definitions".

`eth_typing` holds all types that are generic enough to be useful for other Ethereum related Python libraries. These types are usually very shallow and contain no or only minimal logic. They often fall into the category of `Enum`, `NewType`, `NamedTuple`, `TypedDict` as well as type aliases to specific `Tuple` or `Union` of some types.

The only difference between `eth_typing` and `eth.typing` is that the latter isn't entirely proven to be useful outside of the scope of this repository and so they reside within the repository unless someones screams "Hey, why are we duplicating all these types in project x that also exist in `eth.typing`". Then, we would move them into the `eth_typing` library.

>The role is similar to `eth/rlp/` instead these data structures will be SSZ serialization objects after we finish `py-ssz`

Right, I missed that. It kinda opens up the broader question how does `eth.rlp` relate to this. I guess the fact that they are all RLP serialized and contain some specific functionality makes them not a good fit to go into the broader scope of  `eth_typing`. But apart from that, there may be valid arguments for restructuring our modules to be more like:

```
eth/
    typing/
        rlp/
           eth1
              accounts.py
              blocks.py
              ...
           beacon
              attestation_data.py
              attestation_records.py
              ...
        misc.py (everything that is currently in eth.typing.py)
```

But this opens up an even bigger code organization question. Right now, everything ETH 2.0 related is sitting shielded in `eth/beacon` and while that is great for keeping it isolated from the rest of the library (especially while you keep iterating on the spec heavily) it does make some things less ergonomic I think.

Looking at the following two imports one can easily see that the beacon chain is something that was added later to this library.

```
from eth.rlp.accounts import Account
from eth.beacon.rlp.attestation_data import AttestationData
```

It's just not symmetric. To make it symmetric (and treating `rlp` as a submodule of `typing`) it would either be.

```
from eth.eth1.typing.rlp.accounts import Account
from eth.beacon.typing.rlp.attestation_data import AttestationData
```

or

```
from eth.typing.rlp.eth1.accounts import Account
from eth.typing.rlp.beacon.attestation_data import AttestationData
```

But this opens up a much bigger box of what I initially meant to discuss here (sorry). I think we touched on this topic in https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/930 before. Maybe it would be a good idea to continue the discussion with this specific example at hand. I think that at least *long* term we want to get rid of the asymmetry to not make the beacon support standout alien. /cc @djrtwo @pipermerriam @carver

it has required adding this `ID_LEVEL` to avoid columns name duplication that breaks pandas dictionary groupby (pandas-dev/pandas#50402)

previously, duplicated names appeared due to each `.concat` argument here having exactly the same columns:
```python
>>> pandas.concat([grp.sum(), grp.count()], axis=1)
   b  c  b  c
a
1  3  5  1  1
2  4  6  1  1
3  5  7  1  1
```
and now by specifying `keys` and `names` we can achieve unique column names:
```python
>>> pandas.concat([grp.sum(), grp.count()], axis=1, keys=["sum", "count"], names=["ID_LEVEL"])
ID_LEVEL sum    count
           b  c     b  c
a
1          3  5     1  1
2          4  6     1  1
3          5  7     1  1
```

> You know, it's funny, I've been wondering why west/xyz refs were being tracked in the above branch but didn't chase it down.

The reason for tracking `west/xyz` was PR#188, especially a git does not allow SHAs to be fetched directly, which is described here #139, and therefore we just fetch as PR#140:
```
  if _is_sha(project.revision):
        # Don't fetch a SHA directly, as server may restrict from doing so.
        _git(project, fetch_cmd + ' --tags -- (url)')
        _git(project, 'update-ref (qual-manifest-rev-branch) (revision)')
```
but this only fetch remote HEAD, which is pointing to `master` in this case.
So if the SHA was on a branch, it wouldn't be fetched, which lead to: PR#188.
Where the remote `refs/heads/*` is kept locally under `refs/west/*` and therefore we can checkout any SHA.
You even suggested, just using `refs/west/` https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/west/pull/188#discussion_r254361240
With PR#188, we can checkout any SHA.

> This is technically illegal behavior. We currently promise that we only ever set the manifest_rev ref, 

and `manifest-rev` is still the only head we use under the repos normal refs space: `refs/heads/manifest-rev`, nothing has changed here, and I don't propose to change that.
Only proposal is to keep a local refs space under `refs/west/*`  for anything we fetch, so that we can use that later.

> so the west/ namespace is not available to us.

Technically, you're right. `west` is not using the knowledge that a commit is available under `refs/west/*` directly. `west` just checks out the SHA and points `manifetst-rev` to point to that SHA. It doesn't care whether the SHA is available locally, or remotely.
Actually, you can specify any SHA you have locally in your repo, even one that doesn't exists on the remote side, and `west` will still work, regardsless of whether that SHA is available remote or not.

So if we start using this knowledge in https://github.com/NordicPlayground/fw-nrfconnect-nrf/pull/1437 that is correctly a change in how we use this refs-space.
But as you are only using that refs space `git cat-file` we have not changed the behavior of `west` and we are still not checking out from `refs/west/*`, you are only using it to get information out.

So we are keeping a little more of the remote refs space, locally.
And we are not changing how `west` uses this information, only how an extension command is using that info ;)

It is really down to a zero-compromises approach. Going back to Thread 1.1, the Child Update Request proves a fully functional bi-directional link including security, because the ChildUpdateRequest made it up to the parent, was processed, and the ChildUpdateResponse made it back to the child. The MAC Data request never did that, as a parent will Ack a MAC Data Request even if it can't process the security or doesn't consider it a child. This is why we have the sentence:

> Rx-off-when-idle Children SHOULD also periodically (at time intervals indicated by the application) resynchronize by sending a Child Update Request to their Parent to avoid cases when the MAC Data Request is being acknowledged erroneously by a receiver that is not a valid Parent.

In Thread 1.2, we introduced the Enhanced Frame Pending feature, which allows a child to accomplish the same thing as a MAC Data Request command (more or less) with a MAC Data frame - be aware of when the parent has data. Therefore using a MAC Data frame here is strictly an improvement vs Thread 1.1, whereas making it possible for Rx-On children would be a trade off.

In my case, I have a sklearn pipeline with multiple steps ending with a cox model (wrapped with sklearn-lifelines). The early steps of the pipeline run on data that may have missing survival information. These rows are always dropped before CoxPHFitter is run, but with the new version dropping the rows is not sufficient - the dtype of the column also need to change.

For example: 
```
pipeline = sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ('consensus_cluster', py_utils.cluster_transformer.ClusterTransformer(
            py_utils.consensus_clustering.ConsensusClustering(
                n_clusters=3, base_estimator=sklearn.cluster.AgglomerativeClustering(), n_estimators=1000),
            sklearn.ensemble.RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=1000, random_state=0))),
        ('cox', py_utils.drop_missing_estimator.DropMissingEstimator(
            sklearn_lifelines.estimators_wrappers.CoxPHFitterModel(
                duration_column='overall_survival_months', event_col='has_event')))
    ],
)
```

In this case DropMissingEstimator drops any rows with missing data in them, but is otherwise not specific to CoxPHFitterModel and does not know what the different columns in y are or what dtype they should be.

It is of course not to difficult to make this work with the new lifelines code, but I just wanted to mention it in case this was an unintended consequence of the change.

As far as I could debug manually, google drive either returns:

1. A html stating that the quota is exceeded. We currently parse the contents of this html in `_quota_exceeded`, and yes, size of 128 is sufficient to get to the phrase/part we check against here. Larger chunk sizes didn't hurt in my debugging.
2. The desired payload, e.g. a tarball of a dataset (which cannot be decoded and parsed).

The reason parsing the html is worse than using the return_code is that if google decides to change the formatting of the returned html (or has an edge case where it returns a **different** html to signal something else) our check will fail, we will write html to disk again, and subsequently fail to unpack it in the next step, leaving the users with a cryptic error message.

We could be more strict here by rejecting all responses that have a payload which's first chunk can be decoded to strings, but the best long term solution would probably be for google to fix/adhere to their API.

Assuming we have already completed TLS handshake with the client above in `before_upstream_connection` callback, here we can return response from the cache.  There are two possibilities:

1. Save unencrypted response as cache and encrypt it every time while serving from cache.  In-fact, encryption will be automatic when serving clear text response over the TLS connection.
2. Save encrypted response as cache.  Decrypt to parse it as necessary.  Finally serve data over established TLS connection.  This step involves an edge case that can occur when generated server side certificate expires, resulting into inability to decrypt the stored response or in worst case resulting into serving garbage data to client.

> `--tags` rings a few alarm bells.

Yep. This is the crux of this PR, thanks for finding it right away :smile: .

Context: 

- Nordic's SDK rebases zephyr whenever we cut a new release, to keep a linear history of downstream patches, but allowing `git merge` of upstream zephyr during development.

- We've historically relied on --tags being here to make sure that jumping around between versions during development never creates the possibility for garbage collection of old code, because we tag our history right before we rebase. Running `west update` gets the tag too, so nobody can lose anything.

However, I would be open to dropping --tags here if we can figure out a way to avoid issues.

My main concern is that if I do this:

```
git init
git fetch https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr v2.4.0
git update-ref refs/heads/manifest-rev FETCH_HEAD^{commit}
git checkout --detach manifest-rev
```

Then I have no tags at all, which isn't really what I think we want. I would like to find a solution where fetching a tag gives you a local tag, so you can't lose it later.

Unfortunately I don't think it works to use a byte offset if we're not in binary mode.

For example when we pass an encoding to `io.open()` what we get is a `TextIOBase` in which `seek()` and `tell()` operate in terms of characters rather than bytes:
https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#io.TextIOBase.tell

For consistency, I think if we're in binary mode we want size and offset to represent bytes, and if we're in text mode we want them both to represent characters.  Otherwise we might end up doing text reads that return partial values of multibyte characters, which basically defeats the point of having a text mode.

```suggestion
Version 0.7.0 (January 10, 2019)
--------------------------------
This is a major, API-breaking release. It introduces a large number of new features, bug fixes, and improvements.

API-BREAKING CHANGES:

* A number of entities (or keywords) have been renamed to align more closely with the BIDS specification documents:
  * 'type' becomes 'suffix'
  * 'modality' becomes 'datatype'
  * 'acq' is removed (use 'acquisition')
  * 'mod' becomes 'modality'
* The following directories are no longer indexed by default: derivatives/, code/, stimuli/, models/, sourcedata/. They must be explicitly included using the 'include' initialization argument.
* The grabbids module has been renamed to layout and BIDSLayout.py and BIDSvalidator.py are now layout.py and validation.py, respectively.
* The BIDS validator is now enabled by default at layout initialization (i.e., ``validate=True``\ )
* The ``exclude`` initialization argument has been removed.
* ``BIDSLayout.parse_entities`` utility has been removed (use the more flexible ``parse_file_entities``\ ).
* Calls to ``.get()`` now return ``BIDSFile`` objects, rather than namedtuples, by default (#281). The ``BIDSFile`` API has been tweaked to ensure backwards incompatibility in nearly all cases.
* Naming conventions throughout the codebase have been updated to ensure consistency with the BIDS specs. This is most salient in the ``analysis`` module, where snake_case has been replaced with CamelCase throughout.

NEW FEATURES:

* File metadata (i.e., in JSON sidecars) is now searchable by default, and behaves just like native BIDS entities (e.g., metadata keys can be passed as arguments to ``.get()`` calls)
* A new BIDSFile wrapper provides easy access to ``.metadata`` and ``.image``
* HRF convolution is now supported via bundling of nistats' hemodynamic_models module; convolution is handled via the ``convolve_HRF`` transformation.
* Named config paths that customize how projects are processed can be added at run-time (#313)
* Preliminary support for BIDS-Derivatives RC1 (mainly core keywords)

MINOR IMPROVEMENTS AND BUG FIXES:

* Specifying 'derivatives' in a path specification now automatically includes 'bids' (#246)
* Zenodo DOIs are now minted with new releases (#308)
```

I thought I described the use case already but let me give you another use case where:
1. we don't want to require users to build from source
2. parallel versions need to coexist:
    * neither soname versioning is sufficient to solve it
    * nor is FHS
3. we can't know the final location of the prefix

Consider a CI system creating a snapshot of N packages and making those artifacts available for users to try/use. A user wants to use them (over building from source) since it saves a lot of time - especially on lower end system (1.). A user want to trying different versions of these nightly artifacts and easy switch between them e.g. to figure out if a newer version addresses a certain problem (2.). While we could use a specific unique prefix in every build and require the user to unpack in that specific location that seems to be very restrictive. Since we already rely on environment variable to support parallel versions it is easy to allow unpacking into arbitrary prefixes if the artifact are relocatable (3.).

Btw. ROS does adhere to the FHS for the layout within the chosen prefix (afaik), see [ROS REP 122](https://www.ros.org/reps/rep-0122.html). Not choosing `/usr` stems from the fact that we need to support multiple ROS distros to be installable side-by-side. The separate prefix paths and setting up the necessary env vars for `PATH`, `PYTHONPATH`, `PKG_CONFIG_PATH`, etc. are fairly straight forward and work well for ROS for 8+ years. If every version of every single package would be side-by-side installable with other versions that would not be necessary. But I would argue that looking at the packages available e.g. in Debian / Ubuntu that the case for only very few packages. The majority of packages doesn't support that - e.g. headers are within the same path on the include dir and would collide between versions Yes, packages could do a better job but as a matter of fact the majority of packages doesn't do so. Also keeping in mind that the average developer writing a ROS package has usually no idea of these complex aspects of packaging.

> The blatant and constant violation of the FHS (and the warts growing from that) have been one of the most consistently annoying design problems in ROS, for me and for many other developers I know.

I can only invite you to consider contributing to push things into the direction you think is better (whatever that means). I suggest you do that with concrete suggestions rather than using phrases like the above to express your "disgust". Anything can be changed over time with the right rational and contributions.

---

The goal here is to find the target branch of a PR. In most cases this is `master`, but not in the case of release branches or the occasional branch we spin up temporarily like `apache-parser-v2`. We find this branch and allow this test to pass if certbot-auto's contents are either the same as the branch targeted by the PR or if its contents are the latest released version of the script which allows us to submit PRs properly updating its contents.

In Travis, `TRAVIS_BRANCH` refers to the branch targeted by the PR for PR builds. For non-PR builds `TRAVIS_BRANCH` always referred back to the branch itself so it always passed there.

I'm not sure if Azure has something similar, but my understanding is this variable always refers back to the branch/PR itself which isn't what we want for PR builds.

> and if something were to go wrong with that package on CRAN, you'd get the same kinds of issues as if it were a dependency

Sure, *on CRAN* a `Suggests` dependency imposes the same risks as an `Imports` or `Depends` one since CRAN will install all `Suggests` dependencies when running `R CMD check`.

But putting that into `Suggests` means that for users of the package, `install.packages("lightgbm")` won't require the installation of `{RhcpBLASctl}`, so `{RhcpBLASctl}` being incompatible with their environments wouldn't prevent the use of `{lightgbm}`.

I'm willing to take on a bit more maintenance burden (increased risk of CRAN failures caused by a dependency) in exchange for a better user experience with the package's default values, but I'm not willing to add a new unconditional dependency to `{lightgbm}` just to set a better default value for `num_threads`.

It is a neat pattern huh! Generators are great little state machines that you can ratchet forwards and pass messages to. And of course you can go full functional:

```python
def functional_middleware(app: ASGIApp) -> ASGIApp:
    async def new_app(scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async def tweaked_send() -> "AsyncGenerator[None, Message]":
            message = yield
            headers = Headers(raw=message["headers"])
            should_tweak = headers.get("X-Tweak") == "1"
            await send(message)

            while True:
                message = yield
                if not should_tweak:
                    await send(message)
                else:
                    await send(message)

        async with aclosing(tweaked_send()) as wrapped_send:
            await wrapped_send.__anext__()  # start the generator
            await app(scope, receive, wrapped_send.asend)

    return new_app
```

The PVSyst near shading loss model is broken out into beam, diffuse, and albedo components.  (It's far shading loss is only a beam component.)  It totals these components and applies them as a global shading loss.  Then it applies an Incident Angle Modifier to the beam component.  Then the soiling losses (which have snow losses lumped into them) are applied as a constant haircut to effective irradiance for the entire month.  So, PVSyst is: Shading -> Incident Angle Modifier -> Soiling.

An irradiance_shading_loss_model_pvsyst will need to calculate its global shading loss by accessing the total_irrad['poa_direct'] and total_irrad['poa_diffuse'] parameters.  An alternative implementation to this PR would be to include the shading loss factors when calculating the effective_irradiance_model.

As long as the shading_loss_model properly accounts for each component of the global shading loss and applies this loss as a percentage of total irradiance (as PVSyst does), I don't believe the order of the losses affects the final result.  With this in mind, my objective was to make this PR as small as possible and apply the the shading/soiling/snow losses to the effective irradiance without changing the effective_irradiance_model in PVLib.  Though I'm certainly open to different implementation strategies.

I took a deeper look at the SCPI API and there are roughly 3 modes: 

1. Trigger and return a measurement based on the previously set sense mode (voltage, current, etc.), 
2. Trigger and return a measurement while also specifying the sense mode (which sets the sense mode as a side-effect)
3. Just fetch existing measurements (without triggering, or specifying sense mode)

Additionally, you can request either a single value or an array (where the number of values returned is tied to the number of values used for averaging).

So, given all of the above, I added a whole slew of properties allowing the user to obtain data in a way that fairly closely adheres to the way the SCPI API works.

```suggestion
    Registers routes for a cluster at the edge router.

    registered.
    This method is tightly coupled with `cluster_manager.build_cluster_endpoint`, which already creates the
    endpoint URL according to the configuration used here.
    
    :param host: hostname of the inbound address without scheme or port

    :param forward_url: whole address for outgoing traffic (including the protocol)
```

Previously I only briefly looked at tests but it might be that next time I check it'll be ready for merge so let me highlight a few things.

When writing tests, please think what you want to verify from the tested method. For example, when testing addition operator I not only want to make sure that the output has the same type as inputs but also want to make sure that it actually is adding values. Also, every method has limitations and it'll break and it's important to check that we correctly show these limitations.

Being more specific to your tests:
* Normalize test only checks for length but it actually should check that the signal is normalized. That means checking the mean value being close to zero and norm of amplitude being close to one. When using on normalized signal it should return the same signal.
* Mean period of a sinusoid is well known; you can check for more than just the type of output.
* Significance tests assume `alpha` to be within (0, 1) range. What happens outside that range? It should fail, right? How about `alpha = -10`?

```python
def get_systemd_version():
    """
    Returns systemd's major version, or None if failing to do so.
    """
    try:
        version_response = subprocess.check_output(["systemctl", "--version"])
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError:
        warnings.warn("Failed to call 'systemctl --version', is systemd installed?")
        raise

    try:
        # Example response to parse from Ubuntu 22.04:
        #
        # systemd 249 (249.11-0ubuntu3.9)
        # +PAM +AUDIT +SELINUX +APPARMOR +IMA +SMACK +SECCOMP +GCRYPT +GNUTLS +OPENSSL +ACL +BLKID +CURL +ELFUTILS +FIDO2 +IDN2 -IDN +IPTC +KMOD +LIBCRYPTSETUP +LIBFDISK +PCRE2 -PWQUALITY -P11KIT -QRENCODE +BZIP2 +LZ4 +XZ +ZLIB +ZSTD -XKBCOMMON +UTMP +SYSVINIT default-hierarchy=unified
        #
        version = int(float(version_response.split()[1]))
        return version
    except:
        warnings.warn(f"Failed to parse systemd version from 'systemctl --version' response: {version_response}")
        return None

```

> Meh, I forgot that Graph also accepts a Literal as an identifier, also not captured by `IdentifiedNode`.

It is important to note, this type alias is only used in one place, which is the return value for `Dataset.__iter__` so it should be correct for that, and for this reason it probably should not be a type alias, or at the very least not one outside of the class. At the very least a type alias on module level should be used in more than one class/function in that module before it is clear that it makes sense for it to be an alias, as there may be cases where something looks like a ContextNode and needs to have `Literal`, `str`, `Node` and `None` - and other places where `IdentifiedNode` really is the most appropriate.


Also Literal is not in the value space of graph identifiers/names according to [RDF 1.1](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-dataset):

> An RDF dataset is a collection of [RDF graphs](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph), and comprises:
> 
> - Exactly one default graph, being an [RDF graph](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph). The default graph does not have a name and may be empty.
> - Zero or more named graphs. Each named graph is a pair consisting of an [IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-iri) or a blank node (the graph name), and an [RDF graph](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph). Graph names are unique within an RDF dataset.

So if Graph accepts it as an identifier, it probably should not, and the right thing is to narrow the type there to match the correct return type for `Dataset.__iter__` - if `Dataset.__iter__` does return something that is contrary to the spec I think we should at least understand where things go awry also.

Talked to @Yard1 about this. Here is what's happening with current code:

1. This works for directory-backed checkpoints only. User (or us developers) provide `Checkpoint.from_directory()` to `session.report`. This makes sense because if it fits in memory already, there's technically no need to jump through these hoops.
2. First, upload to cloud with `to_uri`. Note: we need to nail down whose responsibility it is to do syncing. `Syncer` or `Checkpoint`?
3. Next, delete all local paths -- will this result in `checkpoint._local_path` being deleted? Or just an empty folder?
4. Then, still send the checkpoint to the Train driver for the trainable to handle (in code below):

```python
        result = TrainingResult(
            type=TrainingResultType.CHECKPOINT,
            data=checkpoint,
            metadata=metadata,
        )
        self.result_queue.put(result, block=True)
```

5. This is an empty directory at this point?? **Train will hand off this checkpoint to Tune, and it will dump it to checkpoint_0000x and then try uploading to cloud again.**
    <details>User calls session.report(checkpoint) -> _TrainSession.checkpoint -> Driver will pull this TrainingResult from the queue, which loads into memory as bytes, then ships through object store from worker to driver -> **Driver will use `tune.checkpoint_dir()` and `tune.report()` to report an empty checkpoint + rank 0 worker metrics.**/details>

See:

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/dc6fd827b40e987ad35745c4939e6131ae2569f0/python/ray/train/_internal/checkpoint.py#L250-L255

and

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/dc6fd827b40e987ad35745c4939e6131ae2569f0/python/ray/train/data_parallel_trainer.py#L379-L383

6. Tune will handle the checkpoint again. (Ex: deleting a different checkpoint if needed)

### Proposal

Should we pass `None` in the checkpoint `TrainingResult` for Tune to no-op in the case that we already uploaded it?

Other considerations:
- What about `num_to_keep` handling? That is done by Tune and should delete the entire checkpoint_000x folder.

```
if ! service "$service" status 2>&1 | grep -q unrecognized; then
    service "$service" restart
    return $?
else
    return 0
fi
```

```
ssh does not exist in /etc/rc.d or the local startup

1
kanashiro@kanashiro-freebsd-13-debug:~ $ sudo service sshd status
sshd is running as pid 31038.
kanashiro@kanashiro-freebsd-13-debug:~ $ echo $?
0
```

In this case, we are waiting for the previous TCP tx to be done to send the next.
We can consider instead to add the new query to end of the `mSendBuffer`.
TCP provide mechanism to extend the last send buffer using `SendByExtension`. 

We can consider the following:
- If In `kTcpConecting`
   - We update `mSendLinkBuffer`, append new query at the end of previous one (write after `mSendLink.mLength` and update `mSendLink.mLength`)
   - The `mSendLink` will be passed to TCP once we get connection-established callback.
- If in `kTcpSending` (indicates `mSendLink` is in use by TCP)
   - We can append the new content at the end, after `mSendLink.mLength`
   - Then call `SendByExtension` passing in the number of appended bytes.
   - It is important to not change the `mSendLink.mLength` in this case (as `mSendLink` is owned by TCP and it will itself update the length in `SendByExtension`).
- If either case if buffer is full we return `kErrorNoBuf`

Try this example:

```python
from starlette.applications import Starlette
from starlette.middleware import Middleware
from starlette.responses import Response
from starlette.routing import Mount, Route

class PotatoException(Exception):
    ...

async def bad_endpoint(request):
    raise PotatoException

async def exception_handler(request, exc):
    return Response("I have a 400 for you!", status_code=400)

class CustomMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app, name: str):
        self.app = app
        self.name = name

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        async def wrapped_send(msg):
            if msg["type"] = "http.response":
                print(f"{self.name} called for {scope['raw_path']}")
            await send(msg)
        await self.app(scope, receive, wrapped_send)

routes = [
    Mount(
        "/mount",
        routes=[
            Route(
                "/bad",
                endpoint=bad_endpoint,
            )
        ],
        middleware=[Middleware(CustomMiddleware, name="on_mount")],
    ),
    Route(
        "/good",
        endpoint=bad_endpoint,
    )
]

app = Starlette(
    routes=routes,
    exception_handlers={PotatoException: exception_handler},
    middleware=[Middleware(CustomMiddleware, name="on_app")],
)
```

The "on_mount" middleware will never be called because PotatoException tears through `Mount`'s middleware stack. On the other hand "on_app" always gets called because it's "protected" by ErrorMiddleware.

Well, my gut feeling is that it's not worth it: it will still be a very big effort and it will only fix one subtle bug (which doesn't make anything crash) and there will still be some inconsistencies. Let me try to explain... 

* **Current Situation**: If the `category_encoder` is inside a composed `sklearn` transfomer, which delegates the `get_feature_names_out` to every inner estimator, there is an Exception when calling `get_feature_names_out`, because the signature is not valid. The error is `TypeError: get_feature_names_out() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given`. 
* **Super simple Fix**: By changing the signature but not doing anything with the input parameter, we remove this `Exception` and, making using of the attribute `feature_names_out_`, we get a right answer in most situations. I mean, will be right whenever the `category_encoder` is fitted and transformed alone, or whenever is included in a composed transformer but in the first layer. However, if the `category_encoder` is not in the first layer of a composed transformer, and if `set_output` is the default, then the answer will be wrong, because when fitted the previous transformer of the row will output a numpy array, and the `category_encoder` will take ["0", "1", ...] as the original column names. This is the only *issue* that will remain with this super simple fix (and the solution is as easy as setting `set_output=pandas`). 
* **More thorough fix**:  If we modify every encoder by adding a treatment of the input_features in order to generate the column names (which requieres some work for some of the encoders), the only added value will be removing the wrong answer in the mentioned case (when the encoder is part of a composed transformer and has another `sklearn` transfomer doing something before). My point is that, even in this situation, there will be some inconsistences that are related to the fact of assuming that the input is a pandas dataframe. One of those is the fact that some parameters of the encoders depend on column names. For example, you can specify the mapping for an `OrdinalEncoder` using this: 
    ```python
    OrdinalEncoder(mapping=[{'col': 'col_a', 'mapping': {'a': 2, 'b': 1, 'c': 3}}])
    ``` 
    This code will crash in the same situation as before: if the `OrdinalEncoder` is part o a pipeline and there is another `sklearn` transformer before, the columns received during `fit` won't correspond to the columns specified in the `mapping` parameter (unless `set_output=pandas`). You can take a look at this issue [here](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1lJJvXyAOoG9MBtNiH2qSTrqbzZtEPTeB#scrollTo=nCJtM_1keNTD&line=2&uniqifier=1). That's why I was saying that if we wish to be fully aligned with `sklearn`, there will be more things to do (in particular, for example, no column names should be used in any parameter of any encoder). So I feel more inclined to say "all or nothing": if we want full compatibility with `sklearn`, that's fine, but it will be a breaking change a lots of work, and otherwise, I think the *super simple fix* is good enough. Well, that's my opinion...

Thank you for bringing this up.

### Problem
Without looking further into the details and the state of the onion, I was wary of using an Enum-type as key, because I found different resources like those referenced below, which indicate possible performance losses. Especially because the converter sits in a busy place of the driver, I decided to not use it this way and use an `int` as key instead.

### Thoughts
If we still want to use an Enum-type as a key on the mapping data structure itself, I can think of two options, but I will be happy to hear if you see others.
- Use in-memory `@memoize`-style caching on the lookup path, if that would work around the Enum-lookup-is-slow problem.
- Look into using the `fastenum` library.

### References

TLDR; Apparently, Python 3.4's first Enum implementation was pretty slow. While it got better with subsequent releases, the `fastenum` library still claims to be up to 3.5x faster in benchmarks on recent versions of Python.

- https://quantlane.com/blog/fastenum/, https://gitlab.com/quantlane/libs/fastenum
- https://pypi.org/project/fast-enum/
- https://bugs.python.org/issue39102

> Probably I misunderstand something. If during the request we use the `search_pattern`, is the percolator still useful?

The percolate query is used to get *all* sets a record belongs to (needed for the header of the response).
The additional query above is used, when a setSpec is specified in the request (f.e. https://127.0.0.1:5000/oai2d?verb=ListRecords&metadataPrefix=oai_dc&set=my-set). It is used during record querying so only records belonging to this set are retrieved. Then we take these records and do a percolate query to get all the sets the records belong to.

Essentially, we can follow the `pyproj` trick... it seems like a reasonable enough approach to me. Similarly, we can have a `CFUNITS_WHEEL` environment variable which acts as a hook to `setup.py` to instruct it to inject additional `package_data` that consists of the `/usr/share/udunits/*.xml` files (or file, thanks @dopplershift). This will be copied by `setup.py` to `cf_units/etc/share/udunits/*.xml` and shipped in the `wheel`. But this only all happens if the `wheel` generated  `.lib` directory exists as well.

Also, the additional step needed here now is that if no `cf_units/etc/site.cfg` exists **AND** the `cf_units/etc/share/...` exists, then it was a `wheel` install, and `cf-units` can complete the configuration to the XML files at runtime, as it has them bundled and it knows where they are relative to itself (resolved absolutely, hence no need to know the location pre-install, as that's troublesome)... otherwise all bets are off.

I'd opt to bundle the `xml` file/s not in the `.lib` directory, as that seems a bit abusive of the `wheel` mechanism.

As we move forwards, we could adopt this pattern for `conda` too, which means that we could ultimately drop the use of `build.sh` and `build.bat`, right?

The only downside to this is that for legal reasons it's not cool to bundle the `xml` files with `cf-units` as a `wheel`, but I'm hoping that either of you might have thoughts on that...

It 100% isn't a direct service parameter. Based on the inittime_data/inittime_json parameters, we then encapsulate those in an AttestationData model type and then encapsulate _that_ in an AttestationRequest model type. _That_ is the direct service parameter.

My philosophy here has always been to ensure that the data being sent to the service is as valid as possible because checks on the client are orders of magnitude cheaper than checks on the service. So if the client hands us a PEM encoded X.509 certificate, we verify that the PEM value actually contains an X.509 certificate and is valid. And then we generated the JWK needed by the service from the decoded certificate. This does two things: 1) it verifies that the certificate is valid and 2) Ensures that the value sent to the service is canonical (PEM decode is mechanical, but there are a lot of variables and parsing logic that I don't want to have to deal with - I'd rather let some other library do that work).

I have two concerns:

1. The `PYBIND11_EXPORT_EXCEPTION` alternative would mean that we need more involved rearrangements of the existing code. For example this one would get pretty complicated:
```
         if (constexpr_bool(StringCaster::UTF_N == 8) && str_len > 1 && str_len <= 4) {
         ...
         else if (constexpr_bool(StringCaster::UTF_N == 16) && str_len == 2) {
```

Note that the `constexpr_bool` is only for a subexpression.

2. I feel making this look too much like the C++17 `if constexpr` is actually misleading. In my understanding, the primary distinguishing feature is that the body of the `if constexpr` does not participate in template instantiation if the condition is false:

https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/language/if
> If a constexpr if statement appears inside a templated entity, and if condition is not value-dependent after instantiation, the discarded statement is not instantiated when the enclosing template is instantiated .
 
It's actually a very big deal, because it enables much more intuitive and easier to write code than the alternative SFINAE acrobatics we have to resort to before C++17. But clearly, for the existing code that triggers C4127, that distinguishing feature doesn't apply. It just so happens that the condition is a compile-time constant.

> while the warning is a little irritating

I think what is a hack is the implementation behind the warning. I was pretty happy about the minimally obtrusive solution I found. But I'll try something else as soon as I get a chance, more similar to the C4100 approach:

`PYBIND11_SILENCE_MSVC_C4127`

Then it is totally clear why exactly it's there, without getting into the fairly complex territory of `constexpr` semantics.
